[![@jrswab](https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/1689426?u=13a6523aff0f7ae68d14fb9fa708542b9bafd25a&v=4&size=80)](https://github.com/jrswab)

## Description

[![@jrswab](https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/1689426?u=13a6523aff0f7ae68d14fb9fa708542b9bafd25a&v=4&size=48)](https://github.com/jrswab)

[jrswab](https://github.com/jrswab)

opened [on Mar 10, 2026](https://github.com/jrswab/axe/issues/17#issue-4051885222)

Issue body actions

## Summary

The `url_fetch` tool in axe has two significant issues that make it unreliable for production agent runs. The Go implementation is missing functionality that the TypeScript counterpart in agent-engine already handles correctly.

## Issues

### 1\. No per-request HTTP timeout (High)

**File:** `internal/tool/url_fetch.go` (~line 99)

`url_fetch` uses `http.DefaultClient.Do(req)` which has **no timeout configured**. If a target URL hangs without closing the connection, the fetch silently blocks for the entire agent timeout (default 120s) before context cancellation kicks in.

The TypeScript implementation in agent-engine uses `AbortSignal.timeout(15000)` (15 seconds) as a per-request guard.

**Fix:** Add a 15-second per-request timeout using a derived context:

fetchCtx, cancel := context.WithTimeout(ctx, 15\*time.Second)
defer cancel()
req, err := http.NewRequestWithContext(fetchCtx, http.MethodGet, urlStr, nil)

### 2\. No HTML stripping for `text/html` responses (High)

**File:** `internal/tool/url_fetch.go` (~lines 113–126)

The Go implementation returns the raw response body verbatim. For `text/html` responses, this means full HTML markup (including `<script>`, `<style>`, navigation, etc.) is sent to the LLM, wasting tokens and producing noisy, often unusable results.

The TypeScript implementation strips `<script>` blocks, `<style>` blocks, and all remaining HTML tags, returning clean text content.

**Fix:** When the response `Content-Type` is `text/html`, strip:

1.  `<script>...</script>` blocks
2.  `<style>...</style>` blocks
3.  All remaining HTML tags

Then truncate to 10,000 characters (matching the TypeScript implementation's limit).

## Context

All top-level agent runs go through the axe-worker Docker path, so this Go implementation is the production code path. The TypeScript implementation in agent-engine is only used for delegated child agent runs. This means the majority of `url_fetch` invocations hit the Go code with these deficiencies.

Reactions are currently unavailable

Here is the comprehensive overview of the `axe` codebase:

---

## Project Structure

```
/Users/jaronswab/go/src/github.com/jrswab/axe/
├── main.go                        # Entry point: embeds SKILL.md, calls cmd.Execute()
├── go.mod                         # Module: github.com/jrswab/axe, Go 1.24.4
├── cmd/                           # All CLI commands (package cmd)
│   ├── root.go                    # Root cobra command, Execute(), exitCodeFromError()
│   ├── run.go                     # "axe run <agent>" — the core execution path
│   ├── agents.go                  # "axe agents list|show|init|edit"
│   ├── config.go                  # "axe config path|init"
│   ├── gc.go                      # "axe gc" — memory GC with LLM analysis
│   ├── version.go                 # "axe version"
│   ├── exit.go                    # ExitError type
│   ├── args.go                    # exactArgs() validator helper
│   ├── *_test.go                  # Tests for each command
│   └── testdata/
│       ├── agents/                # Fixture TOML configs (basic, with_skill, with_tools, etc.)
│       ├── skills/stub/SKILL.md   # Fixture skill file
│       └── golden/                # Golden output files for dry-run and JSON modes
├── internal/
│   ├── agent/                     # AgentConfig parsing, validation, Load(), List(), Scaffold()
│   ├── config/                    # GlobalConfig (config.toml), API key resolution
│   ├── provider/                  # Provider interface + Anthropic, OpenAI, Ollama, OpenCode impls
│   ├── tool/                      # Tool registry, tool executors, call_agent sub-agent logic
│   ├── toolname/                  # Constants for tool names
│   ├── resolve/                   # Workdir, Files (glob), Skill, Stdin, BuildSystemPrompt
│   ├── memory/                    # Memory file CRUD (FilePath, Append, Load, Trim, Count)
│   ├── mcpclient/                 # MCP server client + router for tool dispatch
│   ├── envinterp/                 # Environment variable interpolation
│   ├── refusal/                   # LLM refusal detection
│   ├── xdg/                       # XDG Base Directory spec helpers (GetConfigDir, GetDataDir)
│   └── testutil/                  # Shared test helpers (SetupXDGDirs, BuildBinary, MockLLMServer, etc.)
├── docs/design/                   # Design docs (agent-config-schema.md, cli-structure.md, etc.)
├── skills/sample/SKILL.md         # Embedded sample skill (served via go:embed)
└── examples/                      # Example agent configurations
```

---

## How Agents Are Configured

Agents are **TOML files** at `$XDG_CONFIG_HOME/axe/agents/<name>.toml`.

**Struct: `agent.AgentConfig`** (`internal/agent/agent.go`):
```go
type AgentConfig struct {
    Name          string            // required
    Description   string
    Model         string            // required — "provider/model-name" format
    SystemPrompt  string
    Skill         string            // path to SKILL.md
    Files         []string          // glob patterns for context files
    Workdir       string
    Tools         []string          // e.g. ["read_file", "list_directory"]
    MCPServers    []MCPServerConfig // MCP server connections
    SubAgents     []string          // names of allowed sub-agents
    SubAgentsConf SubAgentsConfig   // max_depth (≤5), parallel (*bool), timeout
    Memory        MemoryConfig      // enabled, path, last_n, max_entries
    Params        ParamsConfig      // temperature, max_tokens
}
```

**TOML example:**
```toml
name = "pr-reviewer"
model = "anthropic/claude-sonnet-4-20250514"
system_prompt = "You are a code reviewer."
skill = "skills/code-review/SKILL.md"
files = ["src/**/*.go"]
tools = ["read_file", "run_command"]
sub_agents = ["test-runner"]

[memory]
enabled = true
last_n = 10
max_entries = 100

[params]
temperature = 0.3
max_tokens = 4096
```

**Agent lifecycle (`agent.Load()`):**
1. Resolve path: `$XDG_CONFIG_HOME/axe/agents/<name>.toml`
2. Read file via `os.ReadFile`
3. TOML-decode into `AgentConfig` (using `BurntSushi/toml`)
4. Validate: name, model, sub-agent depth limits, tool names, MCP server fields

---

## How Agents Are Executed (`cmd/run.go`)

`axe run <agent>` follows this 21-step pipeline:
1. Load agent TOML (`agent.Load()`)
2. Apply flag overrides: `--model`, `--skill`, `--workdir`
3. Parse model string (`provider/model-name`)
4. Load global config (`config.Load()` — `$XDG_CONFIG_HOME/axe/config.toml`)
5. Resolve working directory (flag > TOML > cwd)
6. Resolve file globs relative to workdir
7. Load skill content from path (3-step fallback: direct → dir/SKILL.md → skills/<name>/SKILL.md)
8. Read stdin (piped or empty)
9. Build system prompt: `system_prompt + skill + context_files`
10. Load memory entries into system prompt (if enabled)
11. **Dry-run mode**: print resolved context and stop (no LLM call)
12. Resolve API key (env var > config.toml > empty)
13. Create provider (`provider.New()`)
14. Build `provider.Request` with model, system, messages, temperature, max_tokens
15. Build tool registry (`tool.RegisterAll()`)
16. Inject `call_agent` tool if sub_agents configured
17. Connect MCP servers, list and register their tools
18. Call provider — either single-shot (no tools) or conversation loop (with tools, up to 50 turns)
19. Execute tool calls in parallel (goroutines) or sequentially
20. Output: plain text to stdout, or `--json` envelope
21. Append memory entry after successful response

**Sub-agent execution** (`internal/tool/tool.go`): when the LLM calls `call_agent`, the tool executor recursively loads and runs the named sub-agent as a full isolated pipeline (its own config, skill, files, memory). Depth is tracked and capped at 3 (default) or up to 5. Results return only final text — internal turns are never surfaced to the parent.

---

## CLI Structure

Built with **cobra** (`github.com/spf13/cobra`). Commands are registered via `init()` functions.

**Command tree:**
```
axe
├── version                    # Print version
├── config
│   ├── path                   # Print XDG config dir
│   └── init                   # Create dir structure + copy sample skill + scaffold config.toml
├── agents
│   ├── list                   # List all valid agent configs
│   ├── show <agent>           # Pretty-print agent config fields
│   ├── init <agent>           # Create scaffolded TOML
│   └── edit <agent>           # Open config in $EDITOR
├── run <agent>                # Execute an agent
│   ├── --skill <path>         # Override skill path
│   ├── --workdir <path>       # Override working directory
│   ├── --model <p/m>          # Override model (provider/model-name)
│   ├── --timeout <int>        # Request timeout in seconds (default 120)
│   ├── --dry-run              # Show resolved context, skip LLM call
│   ├── --verbose / -v         # Print debug info to stderr
│   └── --json                 # Output wrapped JSON envelope
└── gc [agent]                 # Memory GC: analyze + trim agent memory
    ├── --dry-run              # Analyze but don't trim
    ├── --all                  # Run GC on all memory-enabled agents
    └── --model <p/m>          # Override model for GC analysis
```

**Key patterns:**
- `exactArgs(n)` — custom `cobra.PositionalArgs` that generates actionable error messages with extracted placeholder names from `Use` string
- `ExitError{Code, Err}` — wraps errors with exit codes (1=logic, 2=config, 3=provider/auth)
- `SilenceErrors: true`, `SilenceUsage: true` on root — errors printed manually to `os.Stderr` with `fmt.Fprintln`
- All output uses `cmd.OutOrStdout()` / `cmd.ErrOrStderr()` (not bare `os.Stdout`) enabling in-test redirection

---

## Key Interfaces and Types

**`provider.Provider`** (the central interface):
```go
type Provider interface {
    Send(ctx context.Context, req *Request) (*Response, error)
}
```

**`provider.Request` / `provider.Response`** — LLM request/response with `Tools`, `ToolCalls`, `ToolResults` for multi-turn tool use.

**`provider.Tool` / `ToolCall` / `ToolResult`** — Provider-agnostic tool definitions, calls, and results.

**`tool.Registry`** — Map of tool name → `ToolEntry{Definition func, Execute func}`. `RegisterAll()` registers all 7 built-in tools: `list_directory`, `read_file`, `write_file`, `edit_file`, `run_command`, `url_fetch`, `web_search`.

**`tool.ExecContext`** — Carries `Workdir`, `Stderr`, `Verbose` into tool executors (no global state).

**`tool.ExecuteOptions`** — Carries allowed agents, depth, global config, and MCP router into sub-agent invocations.

**`agent.AgentConfig`** — The parsed TOML config (see above).

**`config.GlobalConfig`** — Parsed `config.toml`; provides `ResolveAPIKey(provider)` and `ResolveBaseURL(provider)` with env-var > config file precedence.

**`mcpclient.Router`** — Routes tool dispatch to the correct MCP server client; handles registration with collision detection against built-in tool names.

**`resolve.FileContent`** — `{Path string, Content string}` for resolved glob matches.

**`cmd.ExitError`** — `{Code int, Err error}` maps provider/config errors to specific exit codes.

---

## Testing Patterns

### Unit Tests (internal packages)
- **Table-driven** using `[]struct{name, input, want}` + `t.Run(tt.name, ...)` — used throughout (agent validation, tool dispatch, resolve, memory)
- **No mocks**: use real file I/O with `t.TempDir()` and `t.Setenv("XDG_CONFIG_HOME", tmpDir)` for isolation
- Helper functions: `setupAgentsDir(t)`, `writeAgentFile(t, dir, name, content)` are `t.Helper()`-annotated
- Tests live in the same package (white-box) for internal packages

### Command Tests (`cmd/` package)
- **In-process tests** (`package cmd`): redirect cobra I/O with `rootCmd.SetOut(buf)`, `rootCmd.SetErr(errBuf)`, `rootCmd.SetIn(reader)`, then call `rootCmd.Execute()`
- **Mock HTTP servers** (`net/http/httptest`): `httptest.NewServer` to fake Anthropic/OpenAI/Ollama APIs; each test calls `defer server.Close()`
- `resetRunCmd(t)` resets all flag values between tests (since cobra flag state is shared across tests in-process)
- `setupRunTestAgent(t, name, toml)` — creates temp XDG dir, writes agent TOML, sets env

### Smoke / Integration Tests
- **`testutil.BuildBinary(t)`**: compiles the real binary once (`sync.Once`) per test process, returns path
- **`runAxe(t, env, stdin, args...)`**: spawns the binary as a child process, captures stdout/stderr/exit code
- Useful for testing exit codes and full end-to-end pipeline behavior

### Golden File Tests (`cmd/golden_test.go`)
- Compares command output against files in `cmd/testdata/golden/`
- Supports `--update-golden` flag or `UPDATE_GOLDEN=1` env var to regenerate
- **Masking**: non-deterministic values (workdir paths, `duration_ms`, tool outputs) are replaced with `{{WORKDIR}}`, `{{DURATION_MS}}`, `{{TOOL_OUTPUT}}` before comparison

### `internal/testutil` Package
- `SetupXDGDirs(t)` — creates isolated XDG dirs, sets `XDG_CONFIG_HOME` and `XDG_DATA_HOME` env vars
- `SeedFixtureAgents(t, src, dst)` / `SeedFixtureSkills(t, src, dst)` — copy testdata fixtures into temp dirs
- `MockLLMServer` with `NewMockLLMServer(t, responses)` — queued response mock; fails test if more requests arrive than responses queued
- Factory helpers: `AnthropicResponse()`, `OpenAIResponse()`, `AnthropicToolUseResponse()`, `OpenAIToolCallResponse()`, `SlowResponse(duration)`

---

## Logging / Output Mechanisms

The project rigorously separates stdout from stderr:

| Channel | Used for |
|---------|----------|
| `cmd.OutOrStdout()` / `fmt.Fprint(cmd.OutOrStdout(), ...)` | **Final agent response** (plain or JSON), dry-run output, version, config path, agents list |
| `cmd.ErrOrStderr()` / `fmt.Fprintf(cmd.ErrOrStderr(), ...)` | **Verbose debug info** (`--verbose`): model, workdir, skill, tokens, duration; warnings (memory, MCP), MCP connection logs, sub-agent progress |
| `fmt.Fprintln(os.Stderr, err)` in `Execute()` | Fatal errors at top level |

**Verbose output** (all to stderr):
```
Model:    anthropic/claude-sonnet-4-20250514
Workdir:  /path/to/work
Skill:    skills/sample/SKILL.md
Files:    3 file(s)
Stdin:    yes
Timeout:  120s
Params:   temperature=0.3, max_tokens=4096
Memory:   5 entries loaded from /data/axe/memory/agent.md
[turn 1] Sending request (1 messages, 0 tool calls pending)
[turn 1] Received response: tool_use (2 tool calls)
[mcp] Connecting to "github" at https://... (sse)
[sub-agent] Calling "helper" (depth 1) with task: ...
Duration: 1234ms
Tokens:   100 input, 50 output (cumulative)
Stop:     end_turn
```

parts.text:
Good. I now have all the context needed to form a plan. Let me summarize the issue and present the plan.

---

## Issue Summary

**GitHub Issue #17**: `url_fetch` tool needs two fixes:

1. **No per-request HTTP timeout** — Currently uses `http.DefaultClient.Do(req)` with no timeout. A hanging URL blocks for the entire agent timeout (120s). Should have a 15-second per-request timeout.

2. **No HTML stripping for `text/html` responses** — Raw HTML (scripts, styles, nav, etc.) is returned verbatim, wasting tokens. Should strip `<script>`, `<style>` blocks, then all remaining HTML tags, returning clean text.

## Plan

The changes are scoped to a single file: `internal/tool/url_fetch.go`, with corresponding test additions in `internal/tool/url_fetch_test.go`.

### Pre-implementation analysis

Before coding, per your rules:

1. **Is this even necessary?** — Yes. Both issues are real production concerns: a hanging URL blocks agents for 2 minutes, and raw HTML wastes LLM tokens badly.
2. **What can I remove?** — Nothing to remove. These are additions to existing behavior.
3. **Simplest approach?** — Use `context.WithTimeout` for the timeout (stdlib, zero deps). Use `regexp` for HTML stripping (stdlib, no new dependency needed). The stripping is simple: remove `<script>...</script>`, `<style>...</style>`, then all tags. No need for a full HTML parser.
4. **Now improve** — See implementation details below.

questions.question: For HTML stripping, I see two approaches. Regex-based stripping (remove <script>...</script>, <style>...</style>, then all HTML tags) is simpler and zero-dependency. Alternatively, we could use golang.org/x/net/html for proper DOM parsing and text extraction, which handles edge cases better (e.g., malformed HTML, nested scripts) but adds a dependency. The issue explicitly describes the regex approach matching the TypeScript implementation. Which do you prefer?

options.label: Regex-based (Recommended)
options.description: Use regexp to strip <script>, <style>, and tags. Zero new deps, matches TypeScript implementation, simpler. May mishandle deeply malformed HTML but sufficient for the use case.

# Milestone Plan: url_fetch Tool — Per-Request Timeout & HTML Stripping

**GitHub Issue:** [#17 — url_fetch tool: add per-request timeout and HTML stripping](https://github.com/jrswab/axe/issues/17)  
**Date:** 2026-03-10  
**Branch:** `issue-17-url-fetch-timeout-html-strip` (off `develop`)

---

## Section 1: Research Findings

### Codebase Structure & Relevant Files

The `url_fetch` tool lives entirely within the `internal/tool/` package:

| File | Role |
|------|------|
| `internal/tool/url_fetch.go` (127 lines) | Tool definition + execution logic |
| `internal/tool/url_fetch_test.go` (315 lines) | 13 existing test cases |
| `internal/tool/registry.go` | Registers `url_fetch` via `urlFetchEntry()` |
| `internal/tool/tool.go` | `ToolEntry` type, `ExecContext` struct, `toolVerboseLog` |
| `internal/toolname/` | Constants for tool names (e.g., `toolname.URLFetch`) |

No other packages reference `url_fetch` internals directly. Changes are fully scoped to `internal/tool/`.

### Current Behavior

1. **HTTP client:** `urlFetchExecute` calls `http.DefaultClient.Do(req)` — no per-request timeout. The only timeout protection is the parent `ctx` passed from the agent runner (default 120s).
2. **Response handling:** The raw response body is returned verbatim regardless of `Content-Type`. For `text/html` responses, this means full HTML markup including `<script>`, `<style>`, navigation, ads, etc. are sent to the LLM.
3. **Truncation:** Body is read via `io.LimitReader(resp.Body, maxReadBytes+1)` where `maxReadBytes = 10000`. If the body exceeds 10,000 bytes, it is truncated with a notice appended.
4. **URL sanitization:** Credentials and query strings are stripped from verbose logs via `sanitizeURL()`.

### Key Decisions Made

| Decision | Rationale |
|----------|-----------|
| **15-second per-request timeout** | Matches the TypeScript implementation's `AbortSignal.timeout(15000)`. Long enough for slow servers, short enough to not block an agent run. |
| **Use `golang.org/x/net/html` for HTML stripping** | User chose DOM-based parsing over regex. Handles malformed HTML, nested scripts, and edge cases correctly. Adds one dependency (`golang.org/x/net`) but it's a well-maintained Go sub-repo. |
| **Strip `<script>` and `<style>` elements (including children)** | These elements contain code/CSS that is noise for LLMs. The TypeScript implementation strips these same elements. |
| **Detect `text/html` via `Content-Type` header** | Only strip HTML when the server declares the response as HTML. Non-HTML responses (JSON, plain text, XML) are returned verbatim even if they contain angle brackets. |
| **Truncation applies after stripping** | Stripping reduces content size, so truncating after stripping maximizes useful content within the 10,000 character limit. |
| **`maxReadBytes` (10,000) unchanged** | The existing limit matches the TypeScript implementation and is sufficient for LLM consumption. |

### Approaches Considered & Rejected

| Approach | Why Rejected |
|----------|-------------|
| **Regex-based HTML stripping** (`regexp` stdlib) | User explicitly chose DOM parsing. Regex is fragile with malformed HTML, can't properly handle nested `<script>` tags, and fails on edge cases like `<script>` inside attribute values. |
| **Custom HTTP client with transport-level timeout** | Overly complex. `context.WithTimeout` on the request context is the idiomatic Go approach and achieves the same result with less code. |
| **Configurable timeout per agent** | Out of scope for this issue. The 15-second default matches the TypeScript implementation. Can be added later if needed. |
| **Using `x/net/html` `Tokenizer` (streaming)** | The full `html.Parse` tree approach is simpler to reason about for element skipping (script/style) and produces cleaner code. Performance is not a concern at 10KB max input. |

### Constraints & Assumptions

- **No new public API.** All changes are internal to the `tool` package. `stripHTML` is unexported.
- **Existing tests must pass unchanged.** The 13 existing tests cover: success, empty URL, missing URL, unsupported schemes, HTTP error codes, truncation, context cancellation, connection refused, CallID passthrough, empty body, verbose log sanitization. None of these serve `text/html` content, so they are unaffected by HTML stripping.
- **The existing `TestURLFetch_ContextCancellation` test** uses a 50ms parent timeout. Since `min(50ms, 15s) = 50ms`, the parent timeout still wins — this test continues to pass with the new 15-second per-request timeout.
- **Red/green TDD required.** Tests are written first (red), then implementation (green), per project rules.
- **`golang.org/x/net`** is the only new dependency. It's a Go official sub-repository, widely used, and already transitively available via `golang.org/x/oauth2` in the dependency tree.
- **Whitespace collapsing.** After extracting text nodes from the DOM, consecutive whitespace should be collapsed to single spaces and the result trimmed. This prevents large runs of blank lines from wasting the character budget.

### Open Questions & Answers

| # | Question | Answer |
|---|----------|--------|
| 1 | Should the timeout be configurable via TOML? | **No.** Out of scope. The 15-second hardcoded default matches the TypeScript implementation. Can be revisited in a future issue. |
| 2 | Regex or DOM parser for HTML stripping? | **DOM parser** (`golang.org/x/net/html`). User explicitly chose this approach for correctness with malformed HTML. |
| 3 | Should `<noscript>` content be preserved? | **Yes.** Only `<script>` and `<style>` are stripped. `<noscript>` often contains meaningful fallback text. |
| 4 | What about `Content-Type: text/html; charset=utf-8`? | The check uses `strings.HasPrefix` (or `mime.ParseMediaType`) on the Content-Type header, so charset parameters are handled correctly. |
| 5 | Should HTML stripping apply to error responses (non-2xx)? | **Yes.** Error pages are often HTML too. Stripping before returning the error body keeps error messages clean. |

---

## Section 2: Milestones

- [ ] 001: Add 15-second per-request HTTP timeout to `url_fetch` tool
- [ ] 002: Add HTML stripping for `text/html` responses using `golang.org/x/net/html`
- [ ] 003: Verify all existing and new tests pass, run vet/lint checks
